metadata.title: Create Event Details Panel (Read-Only for Tier 1)

story.iWant: to inspect the currently selected event in a read-only side panel
story.soThat: I can see full event details without leaving the calendar shell
story.tasks: - Extend the shared calendar display model and query path so the panel has all required display fields, including a colour display name - Create EventDetailsPanelViewModel that subscribes to EventSelectedMessage and exposes read-only panel state - Create EventDetailsPanelControl with right-side layout, close button, disabled Edit button, and presentation-only open/close animation - Host the panel in MainPage as a sibling overlay to CalendarFrame so it survives Year/Month/Week/Day page navigation - Reuse ICalendarSelectionService for close behavior and Escape handling instead of adding a second selection state path - Add unit tests for view-model behavior and extend colour-service tests if shared colour-name lookup is added here

acceptanceCriteria.ac: Given an event is selected through ICalendarSelectionService, the details panel appears on the right side of the main calendar shell and completes its slide-in or open transition within 200 ms.

Given the panel is open, it displays the selected event's title, local start/end date-time, colour swatch, colour name, description area, source label "From Google Calendar", and last-synced timestamp.
Given the panel is open, the Edit button is visible but disabled, with tooltip text "Coming in Tier 2", and clicking it takes no action.
Given the panel is open, clicking the close button or pressing Esc clears selection through ICalendarSelectionService.ClearSelection(), and the panel closes.
Given the panel is open, switching Year, Month, Week, or Day view does not clear selection and the same event remains displayed.
Given the panel is open, when EventSelectedMessage(null) is published, the panel closes and clears its visible content state.
Given the selected event has null or unknown optional data such as Description, LastSyncedAt, or colour metadata, the panel renders fallback values without throwing or showing broken layout.

doc.title: Story 3.4 (primary reference)
doc.snippet: Repo-accurate implementation guide for the read-only details panel, including the MainPage overlay requirement, reuse of the existing selection/message services, and the current colour-mapping gap.

doc.title: Epic 3 Technical Specification
doc.section: Detailed Design, Workflows, Acceptance Criteria, NFRs
doc.snippet: Primary product-level source for Story 3.4 behavior: right-side details panel, non-modal interaction, Esc-to-close, persistence across view switches, and performance target under 200 ms.

doc.title: Epic Breakdown
doc.snippet: Confirms the user story, Tier 1 read-only scope, panel width guidance, and dependency on Story 3.3 event selection.

doc.title: UX Design Specification
doc.section: Tier 2 detail panel pattern, navigation patterns, responsive layout
doc.snippet: The right-side detail panel is a fixed-width desktop rail with Escape-to-close behavior. Even though Story 3.4 is read-only, it should lay the structural groundwork for the Tier 2 editable panel.

doc.title: Color Definitions
doc.snippet: Authoritative source for human-readable colour names. Azure is the only currently implemented mapping in code, but this document defines the intended display names for the broader taxonomy.

doc.title: Story 3.3
doc.section: Dev Notes - Actual contracts and Esc handling
doc.snippet: Important dependency context: ICalendarSelectionService, CalendarSelectionService, and EventSelectedMessage already exist and must be reused rather than recreated for the panel workflow.

file.reason: Main calendar shell that owns CalendarFrame. EventDetailsPanelControl must be hosted here as a sibling overlay so the panel persists across frame navigation.
Contains the existing keyboard-accelerator setup and view-mode switching. Add Escape handling here so panel close reuses the same global selection-clear path as Story 3.3.
New view model for the read-only details panel. Owns panel visibility, formatted display fields, and messenger-driven selection handling.
New right-side panel view. Responsible for read-only layout, disabled Edit button, close affordance, and the presentation layer of the open/close animation.
Use only for DI/DataContext hookup and presentation-only animation or focus plumbing. Do not move query or selection logic here.
The current shared UI model lacks a colour display name. Extend it here rather than inventing a panel-only parallel model unless implementation proves a separate details DTO is clearly better.
Already exposes GetEventByGcalIdAsync(string ...). Reuse this contract for panel loads; do not add a second event-details query path without need.
Current mapping already handles UTC-to-local conversion and LastSyncedAt. Extend the projection here if the details panel needs more display metadata such as colour name.
The repo currently exposes only GetHexColor. Story 3.4 may need a small shared extension so the panel can render a colour name without duplicating lookup logic.
Currently the Azure-only stub from Story 3.1. If Story 3.2 has not been implemented yet, make the smallest compatible change here and keep the mapping centralized.
Selection ownership already lives here. Both the panel close button and Escape handling must route through ClearSelection() to keep selection and panel state synchronized.
Publishes EventSelectedMessage and suppresses duplicate same-ID selections. The panel should expect message-driven state transitions from this service.
Existing messenger contract for open and close behavior: non-null ID opens or refreshes the panel; null closes it.
Source entity for title, description, timestamps, colour ID, recurrence flags, and LastSyncedAt. GcalEventId is the actual string primary key.
Confirms gcal_event_id is the PK and last_synced_at is mapped directly on GcalEvent. Use this instead of stale planning assumptions about integer IDs or DataSourceRefresh lookups.
New test file for messenger-driven load and close behavior, null-data fallbacks, and close-command integration with ICalendarSelectionService.
If Story 3.4 extends the colour service contract, update the existing tests here instead of creating a second colour-service test file.

package.name: Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Sqlite
package.use: Existing query/repository stack; no direct DB access from the panel

package.name: Microsoft.WindowsAppSDK
package.use: WinUI 3 views, keyboard accelerators, animations, layout controls

package.name: Moq
package.use: Mock ICalendarQueryService and ICalendarSelectionService in EventDetailsPanelViewModel tests

package.name: FluentAssertions
package.use: Readable assertions in new unit tests

dotnet.newPackagesNeeded: None. This story should build on the packages already referenced by the application and test projects.

constraints.constraint: The repository is flat at the project root. New files belong under Views/ and ViewModels/ at the root. Do not create Core/, src/, or a second UI assembly for this story.

EventDetailsPanelControl must be hosted in MainPage, not inside YearViewControl, MonthViewControl, WeekViewControl, or DayViewControl, otherwise the panel will be destroyed by frame navigation and AC-3.4.5 will fail.
Close behavior must flow through ICalendarSelectionService.ClearSelection() and EventSelectedMessage(null). Do not add a separate local boolean-only state machine for panel closing.
GcalEventId is a string PK. Never use int event IDs in view models, tests, messages, or service calls for this story.
Reuse ICalendarQueryService.GetEventByGcalIdAsync(...) for panel loads. Do not bypass the repository stack with direct CalendarDbContext queries from the panel or its view model.
CalendarQueryService already converts UTC timestamps to local display values. Do not apply a second local-time conversion in the view or view model.
Do not create a panel-specific colour dictionary. If more metadata than GetHexColor is needed, extend the shared IColorMappingService path or consume Story 3.2's implementation once available.
Story 3.4 remains read-only. The Edit button is visible only as disabled scaffolding for Story 3.5; do not implement editing, auto-save, validation, or event persistence here.

The repo currently has no reusable BoolToVisibilityConverter or StringToVisibilityConverter. Prefer explicit bindable visibility properties over adding converter infrastructure unless there is a clear broader reuse case.
Code-behind may handle DI/DataContext hookup, keyboard wiring, and presentation-only animation. Business logic, selection logic, and query logic stay in the view model and services.
Story 3.3 contracts already exist in code, but its UI wiring is still a separate story concern. Story 3.4 must reuse those contracts without reimplementing Story 3.3 inside the panel.

tests.standards: xUnit plus FluentAssertions for unit tests. Use Moq for service doubles, following the style already established in MainViewModelTests and SettingsViewModelTests.

ideas.test: Mock ICalendarQueryService to return a display model with title, local time range, colour metadata, description, and LastSyncedAt. Publish EventSelectedMessage("evt-1") and assert the view model exposes visible panel state and formatted display values.
Publish EventSelectedMessage(null) and assert IsPanelVisible becomes false and panel-facing properties reset to their hidden-state values.
Execute CloseCommand and verify ICalendarSelectionService.ClearSelection() is called exactly once.
Return a display model with null or whitespace description and assert DescriptionDisplay becomes a placeholder such as "No description provided."
Return a display model with LastSyncedAt null and assert LastSyncedDisplay equals "Never".
Mock ICalendarQueryService.GetEventByGcalIdAsync to return null for a selected ID and assert no exception is thrown and the panel remains hidden or safely cleared.
Open the panel once, then simulate unrelated shell state changes without publishing EventSelectedMessage(null). Assert the panel view model retains the selected event state.
If Story 3.4 adds shared colour-name lookup, verify null and unknown colour IDs still resolve to Azure fallback values without throwing.
